one_line_takeaway: A first empirical, feature- and performance-based benchmark of commercial/open-source LLM prompt-injection/jailbreak guardrails finds that raw LLM baselines are unusable due to high false-positive rates and that even the best bolt-on tools (Lakera Guard, ProtectAI LLM Guard) trade off usability against detection performance, with none dominating across precision, recall, and latency.
key_finding: Across all three test conditions the ChatGPT-3.5-Turbo baseline had the highest raw accuracy/precision/recall but an unusably high false-positive rate (FPR 0.431-0.859), i.e., it over-flags benign prompts as malicious and is not viable as a standalone safety filter. With no context, Lakera Guard was best overall: precision 0.964, recall 0.501, FPR 0.057, latency 0.066s, though accuracy only 60.80%; Azure Prompt Shield and Calypso AI Moderator had comparable precision but much higher latency (0.349s and 0.25s). Rebuff reached the second-highest accuracy (71.40%, F1 0.816) but had FPR 0.68 and the worst latency (29.33s/prompt). With system-prompt context prefixed, Lakera Guard again led (accuracy 74.6%, precision 0.94, FPR rising to 0.14), while Rebuff became unusable (3-5 minute per-prompt timeouts) and Vigil was the only tool whose latency dropped with added context. On the Deepset dataset, Lakera Guard achieved its best results (accuracy 87.91%, precision 0.984, recall 0.707, F1 0.823, FPR 0.007, latency 0.051s), and all tools performed better than on the paper's own harder benchmark, which the authors attribute to Deepset's simpler injection techniques and possible train/test leakage (tools may have been trained on that public dataset). Per-attack-source ASR (Tables 6-7) showed the baseline model's ASR rose from 63% (manual prompts, no context) to 100% (manual prompts, with context) - i.e., the baseline blocked zero manually crafted attacks once context was added - while ASR against Garak/PromptMap-generated prompts fell to near 0% for several tools once context was added, indicating detection efficacy is strongly dependent on how the attack prompt was generated, not just the tool used. Overall conclusion: guardrail providers' efforts are insufficient; Lakera Guard and ProtectAI LLM Guard emerged as the best overall tools illustrating a usability/performance tradeoff, and no tool dominates on precision, recall, and latency simultaneously.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is (1) a market survey identifying 13 LLM 'bolt-on' security tools (9 closed-source, 4 open-source) that filter LLM inputs/outputs, of which only 7 could actually be evaluated due to non-participation by proprietary vendors; (2) the Palit Benchmark, a purpose-built dataset of 500 prompts (75.8% malicious, 24.2% benign) spanning 4 mock LLM-integrated applications (French translation, Finance chatbot, Code/SQL-generation tool, general Chat assistant), combining hand-crafted prompts, automated attacks from Houyi, Garak, and PromptMap, plus augmentation from the public Deepset prompt-injections dataset; and (3) an empirical comparative evaluation of these tools (and a ChatGPT-3.5-Turbo baseline used as a naive LLM classifier) using standard detection metrics (precision, recall, accuracy, FPR, F1, latency, Attack Success Rate) across three conditions: benchmark prompts without system-prompt context, benchmark prompts with system-prompt context prefixed, and the public Deepset dataset.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only chat-style or LLM-integrated-application access (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Copilot, or a banking/support chatbot) and no knowledge of model architecture, parameters, or training data. The paper explicitly excludes grey/white-box attacks (model poisoning, backdooring, adversarial-suffix/gradient-based attacks such as Zou et al.'s GCG) as out of scope, citing Apruzzese et al.'s cost-driven threat-model argument that real-world attackers overwhelmingly use primitive rather than adversarial techniques. Attacker goals are framed as classic CIA-triad attacks on LLM systems: integrity (prompt injection to alter output/behavior), availability (DoS via resource-exhausting or malformed inputs), and privacy (extracting sensitive/PII data or training data). Only word- and sentence-level prompt manipulations (not embedding- or token-level attacks) are considered feasible under this black-box, low-resource threat model.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) attacks were confined to word/sentence-level manipulation, excluding embedding- and token-level attack spaces due to compute constraints, so representativeness versus a resourced attacker is unclear; (2) the benchmark is not calibrated to real-world attacker behavior, being inspired by researcher-created attack taxonomies (Houyi, Garak, PromptMap) rather than threat intelligence; (3) the dataset is deliberately imbalanced (75.8% malicious) which inverts real-world traffic distribution and can favor metrics like accuracy in misleading ways; (4) only 7 of 13 identified tools (and only 4 of 9 proprietary tools) could be evaluated because most closed-source vendors did not respond to trial/access requests, so the study gives an incomplete picture of the market; (5) low statistical power for the per-attack-type ASR breakdown (Tables 6-7) is explicitly flagged by the authors; (6) potential data leakage/overfitting on the Deepset dataset, since some tools may have been trained on it, inflating their measured performance relative to the paper's own benchmark.

datasets_used: Palit Benchmark Dataset (this paper's own): 500 prompts, 75.8% malicious / 24.2% benign, across 4 baseline LLM applications (French translation, Finance, Code/SQL, Chat), combining hand-crafted prompts and automated attacks from Houyi, Garak, and PromptMap
Deepset Prompt Injections Dataset (external, from Deepset.AI): 399 benign, 263 malicious prompts, used both to augment the benchmark and as an independent public evaluation set
quantitative_results: No-context benchmark: Lakera Guard precision 0.964, recall 0.501, FPR 0.057, F1 0.659, latency 0.066s, accuracy 60.80%
No-context benchmark: Baseline (ChatGPT-3.5-Turbo) accuracy 84.80%, precision 0.87, recall 0.939, FPR 0.438, F1 0.903, latency 0.35s
No-context benchmark: Rebuff accuracy 71.40%, F1 0.816, FPR 0.68, latency 29.33s
No-context benchmark: LangKit Canary Scanner accuracy 34.20%, FPR 0; LangKit Similarity Scanner accuracy 38.40%, FPR 0.099, latency 0.035s (lowest latency)
With-context benchmark: Baseline accuracy 78.40%, precision 0.782, recall 0.989, FPR 0.859, F1 0.874, latency 0.753s
With-context benchmark: Lakera Guard accuracy 74.6% (best among tools), precision 0.94, FPR 0.14
With-context benchmark: Rebuff experienced unresolvable timeouts (3-5 minutes per prompt)
Deepset dataset: Lakera Guard accuracy 87.91%, precision 0.984, recall 0.707, FPR 0.007, F1 0.823, latency 0.051s
Deepset dataset: Baseline accuracy 72%, precision 0.592, recall 0.95, FPR 0.431, F1 0.729, latency 0.406s
Deepset dataset: Azure Prompt Shield precision 1.0, recall 0.389, F1 0.56
ASR without base prompt: Baseline ASR 62.96% on manual prompts, 4.13% on Houyi prompts, 6.78% on Garak prompts, 13.56% on PromptMap prompts
ASR with base prompt prefixed: Baseline ASR rose to 100.00% on manual prompts (blocked zero attacks) and dropped to 0.00% on Garak prompts
Prompt entropy: PromptMap-generated prompts had highest average entropy (4.524); manually generated prompts lowest (4.165)
Market survey: 13 tools identified fitting inclusion criteria (9 closed-source, 4 open-source); only 7 tools evaluated; only 4 of 9 proprietary providers offered free trials
baselines_compared: ChatGPT-3.5-Turbo (used as a naive prompt classifier baseline with a sandwich-defense system prompt)
Vigil / DeadbitsAI (open-source, semantic similarity + gelectra-base-injection transformer)
